title: Datastore Capacity Trending
spl:
index=vmware sourcetype="vmware:inv:datastore"
| eval used_pct = round((capacity - freeSpace) / capacity * 100, 1)
| stats latest(used_pct) as used_pct, latest(freeSpace) as free_GB by name
| eval free_GB = round(free_GB / 1073741824, 1)
| where used_pct > 80
| sort -used_pct
description: A full datastore prevents VM disk writes, causing crashes and corruption. Datastores fill gradually from VM growth, snapshots, and log accumulation.
value: A full datastore prevents VM disk writes, causing crashes and corruption. Datastores fill gradually from VM growth, snapshots, and log accumulation.
implementation: Splunk_TA_vmware collects datastore inventory automatically. Set alerts at 80% (warning), 90% (high), 95% (critical). Use `predict` command for 30-day forecasting. Include snapshot size in the analysis.
visualization: Gauge per datastore, Table (name, capacity, free, % used), Line chart with predict trendline.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` avg(Performance.storage_used_percent) as disk_pct
  from datamodel=Performance where nodename=Performance.Storage
  by Performance.host Performance.mount span=1h
| where disk_pct > 85
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
ŌĆó Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_vmware`.
ŌĆó Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=vmware:perf:datastore` or `sourcetype=vmware:inv:datastore`.
ŌĆó For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ŌĆö Configure data collection
Splunk_TA_vmware collects datastore inventory automatically. Set alerts at 80% (warning), 90% (high), 95% (critical). Use `predict` command for 30-day forecasting. Include snapshot size in the analysis.

Step 2 ŌĆö Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=vmware sourcetype="vmware:inv:datastore"
| eval used_pct = round((capacity - freeSpace) / capacity * 100, 1)
| stats latest(used_pct) as used_pct, latest(freeSpace) as free_GB by name
| eval free_GB = round(free_GB / 1073741824, 1)
| where used_pct > 80
| sort -used_pct
```

Understanding this SPL

**Datastore Capacity Trending** ŌĆö A full datastore prevents VM disk writes, causing crashes and corruption. Datastores fill gradually from VM growth, snapshots, and log accumulation.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=vmware:perf:datastore` or `sourcetype=vmware:inv:datastore`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_vmware`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedŌĆörename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: vmware; **sourcetype**: vmware:inv:datastore. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

ŌĆó Scopes the data: index=vmware, sourcetype="vmware:inv:datastore". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
ŌĆó `eval` defines or adjusts **used_pct** ŌĆö often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
ŌĆó `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
ŌĆó `eval` defines or adjusts **free_GB** ŌĆö often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
ŌĆó Filters the current rows with `where used_pct > 80` ŌĆö typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
ŌĆó Orders rows with `sort` ŌĆö combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` avg(Performance.storage_used_percent) as disk_pct
  from datamodel=Performance where nodename=Performance.Storage
  by Performance.host Performance.mount span=1h
| where disk_pct > 85
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Datastore Capacity Trending** ŌĆö A full datastore prevents VM disk writes, causing crashes and corruption. Datastores fill gradually from VM growth, snapshots, and log accumulation.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=vmware:perf:datastore` or `sourcetype=vmware:inv:datastore`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_vmware`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedŌĆörename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

ŌĆó Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Performance` ŌĆö enable acceleration for that model.
ŌĆó Filters the current rows with `where disk_pct > 85` ŌĆö typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Step 3 ŌĆö Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Gauge per datastore, Table (name, capacity, free, % used), Line chart with predict trendline.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on datastore Capacity Trending and raise the alarm before it drags down real work or real outages start.
knownFalsePositives: Storage growth and capacity signals can track expected backups, storage vMotion, or provisioning tests; align spikes with change calendars and storage operations.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable data model acceleration for the CIM Performance data model; align field aliases to the same dataset the example uses (here: Performance).
